content.assessment: This fiscal-lever proposal operates upstream of student-level psychological mechanisms, targeting institutional incentives rather than selection structure, admissions process, or classroom environment directly. Because it does not alter selection timing, track structure, or admissions criteria per se, it has no direct first-order effect on big-fish-little-pond, labeling, goal-orientation, or self-determination dynamics as experienced by students. Any psychological effects would be mediated through second-order changes in school composition if maintainers respond to funding incentives by broadening access — a causal chain the proposal itself acknowledges is unproven. This lens has limited purchase on fiscal-instrument design and cannot assess its likely effectiveness at shifting the psychologically relevant sorting mechanisms.
opportunities: Existing ABC testing infrastructure could, in principle, be repurposed to track downstream psychological indicators (e.g., self-concept, motivation) alongside diversity metrics if extended
strengths: If effective at broadening cohort diversity, could indirectly reduce the concentration effects underlying big-fish-little-pond dynamics by changing school composition without requiring direct admissions mandates
threats: Maintainers could respond to funding pressure via alternative non-SES admission criteria that create new, unstudied labeling or exclusion dynamics for other student groups
Symbolic rather than binding implementation, as flagged in the proposal's own risk analysis, would mean no actual change to the psychological conditions of selection despite appearing responsive
weaknesses: No direct mechanism addresses selection timing, track structure, or admissions criteria, leaving the primary drivers of reference-group and labeling effects unaddressed at the source
